does: This paper introduces Terms-Bench, a diagnostic framework for evaluating large language model negotiation agents that moves beyond simple metrics like deal rate. The framework uses a Bayesian-game setup where the counterpart's latent type, policy, and payoff structure are fully specified and observable to the evaluator but hidden from the agent being tested. In bilateral price negotiation scenarios, this design transforms the counterpart into a diagnostic instrument that reveals where and why agents fail by comparing their behavior against oracle-optimal policies. The framework enables fine-grained attribution of negotiation failures to specific agent deficiencies rather than aggregating performance into opaque outcome metrics.
connects: This work extends a line of research on agentic evaluation for language models, responding to limitations in existing negotiation benchmarks that rely on LLM-versus-LLM dynamics or coarse outcome measures. It builds on negotiation theory and mechanism design, which have long used Bayesian-game frameworks to model strategic interaction under incomplete information. The paper also connects to broader efforts in AI evaluation to move beyond task-agnostic metrics toward interpretable, verifiable assessment methods. Prior work on game-theoretic evaluation of agents and principled testing methodologies for strategic reasoning inform the approach of using a well-specified counterpart policy as a reference point.
enables: This diagnostic framework opens new research directions in understanding how LLMs handle strategic reasoning, communication under uncertainty, and incentive alignment. It enables the study of which linguistic patterns, reasoning strategies, or prompt designs improve negotiation outcomes, and allows systematic analysis of how agents fail to extract value or reach mutually beneficial agreements. The approach can extend beyond price negotiation to other structured strategic settings like contract negotiation, resource allocation, or multi-issue bargaining. The framework also supports development of improved training methods by identifying specific weaknesses in agent behavior, potentially leading to more robust negotiation capabilities and better understanding of when and how language models can reason about hidden preferences and strategic interaction.
